name: Participant Inclusion Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Increased participant yield → Systemic: Improved study efficiency and data quality → Strategic: Enhanced credibility of research findings
options: Recruit participants through sleep clinics only
Utilize online advertising with targeted demographics
Implement a comprehensive community outreach program to engage potential participants
review: Controls recruitment costs vs. participant yield. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact on participant privacy and informed consent.

name: Sensor Acquisition Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Reduced data noise → Systemic: Improved sensor accuracy and reliability → Strategic: Enhanced ability to detect rare parasomnia events
options: Utilize a single type of sensing technology (e.g., EEG headbands)
Implement a tiered acquisition model with escalating levels of sensitivity
Employ a hybrid approach combining multiple sensing technologies
review: Controls sensor costs vs. data quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact on participant comfort and sleep quality.

name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development
consequences: Immediate: Reduced manual review burden → Systemic: Improved event-triage efficiency and accuracy → Strategic: Enhanced ability to detect rare parasomnia events
options: Develop a simple, rule-based algorithm
Implement a machine learning-based approach with continuous training
Employ a hybrid approach combining human expertise with machine learning
review: Controls algorithm complexity vs. event-triage efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact on participant safety and data quality.

name: Data Sharing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Increased collaboration opportunities → Systemic: Improved data sharing and reuse → Strategic: Enhanced credibility of research findings
options: Share de-identified physiological data only
Publish raw sensor data with participant consent
Make all study data, including participant information, publicly available
review: Controls data privacy vs. collaboration opportunities. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact on participant trust and informed consent.

name: Budget Allocation Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Reduced costs → Systemic: Improved budget efficiency and effectiveness → Strategic: Enhanced ability to achieve research goals
options: Allocate a fixed percentage of the budget to each research aim
Prioritize funding for research aims with the highest potential impact
Implement a dynamic budget allocation strategy based on project progress
review: Controls budget allocations vs. research outcomes. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact on participant compensation and staff salaries.

name: Participant Screening Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Reduced participant dropout → Systemic: Improved data quality and reliability → Strategic: Enhanced scientific rigor and credibility.
options: Conduct thorough clinical interviews with participants' bed partners or household members to validate self-reported parasomnia episodes.
Implement a tiered screening process, starting with basic questionnaires and escalating to more comprehensive assessments for high-risk participants.
Utilize machine learning algorithms to analyze sensor data and identify potential parasomnia events in real-time, triggering further clinical evaluation.
review: Controls participant selection vs. researcher bias. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of participant fatigue on screening outcomes.

name: Sensor Calibration Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Improved sensor accuracy → Systemic: Enhanced data quality and reliability → Strategic: Increased scientific credibility and comparability.
options: Develop a standardized calibration protocol for all sensors, ensuring consistency across participants and study periods.
Implement regular sensor maintenance and replacement schedules to minimize equipment failure and data loss.
Integrate real-time sensor recalibration capabilities into the monitoring system, allowing for automatic adjustments based on changing participant conditions.
review: Controls equipment quality vs. researcher expertise. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of environmental factors on sensor accuracy.

name: Data Annotation Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Improved data annotation efficiency → Systemic: Enhanced data quality and reliability → Strategic: Increased scientific productivity and collaboration.
options: Develop a comprehensive annotation guide for researchers, outlining clear criteria for identifying parasomnia events.
Implement a collaborative annotation process, where multiple researchers review and discuss annotations to ensure consistency and accuracy.
Utilize machine learning algorithms to automate data annotation, reducing the burden on human annotators and improving overall efficiency.
review: Controls annotation quality vs. researcher agreement. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of inter-rater reliability on study outcomes.

name: Participant Compensation Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Improved participant engagement and retention → Systemic: Enhanced data quality and reliability → Strategic: Increased scientific credibility and comparability.
options: Offer competitive compensation packages to participants, including per-night stipends and meal allowances.
Implement a tiered compensation system, providing higher incentives for participants who complete longer study periods or provide high-quality data.
Utilize cryptocurrency or other alternative reward systems to incentivize participant participation and engagement.
review: Controls participant motivation vs. researcher budget. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of participant demographics on compensation outcomes.

name: Researcher Training Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Improved researcher proficiency → Systemic: Enhanced data quality and reliability → Strategic: Increased scientific credibility and comparability.
options: Develop comprehensive training programs for researchers, covering topics such as parasomnia diagnosis, sensor operation, and data annotation.
Implement regular researcher performance evaluations, providing feedback and coaching to improve skills and knowledge.
Utilize online learning platforms or other digital resources to provide researchers with just-in-time training and support.
review: Controls researcher expertise vs. study quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of researcher turnover on study outcomes.

name: Data Sharing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Improved data accessibility → Systemic: Enhanced scientific collaboration and productivity → Strategic: Increased scientific credibility and comparability.
options: Develop a clear data sharing policy, outlining the types of data that can be shared and with whom.
Implement a secure data repository, providing researchers with access to de-identified participant data for approved research purposes.
Utilize blockchain or other distributed ledger technologies to ensure the integrity and provenance of shared data.
review: Controls data security vs. researcher collaboration. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of data sharing on participant privacy.

name: Governance Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Improved governance and oversight → Systemic: Enhanced study quality and reliability → Strategic: Increased scientific credibility and comparability.
options: Establish a clear governance structure, outlining the roles and responsibilities of key stakeholders such as the PI, board members, and researchers.
Implement regular progress reviews and evaluations, ensuring that the study is on track to meet its objectives.
Utilize machine learning algorithms or other advanced analytics tools to provide real-time monitoring and feedback on study performance.
review: Controls governance quality vs. researcher accountability. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of governance on participant engagement and retention.

name: Participant Flow Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Efficient participant recruitment → Systemic: Reduced participant dropout → Strategic: Improved data quality.
options: Structured pre-admission pathway with specialist clinical interview and collateral history
Phased participant enrollment with initial pilot cohort, followed by expanded recruitment channels
Innovative gamified consent process leveraging interactive digital tools
review: Controls Participant Flow vs. Data Quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact of participant fatigue on data quality.

name: Sensor Calibration Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Improved sensor accuracy → Systemic: Enhanced event detection → Strategic: Increased data reliability.
options: Standardized calibration procedures with regular sensor checks
Advanced machine learning-based sensor calibration for optimal performance
In-situ sensor recalibration during participant stays to adapt to changing environmental conditions
review: Controls Sensor Accuracy vs. Participant Comfort. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact of sensor recalibration on participant sleep patterns.

name: Data Sharing Governance Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Enhanced data security → Systemic: Increased transparency → Strategic: Improved collaboration.
options: Strict de-identification protocols with limited public data sharing
Conditional data access with participant consent and researcher approval
Open-data policy with public repository and community-driven annotation
review: Controls Data Access vs. Researcher Collaboration. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact of open-data policies on participant privacy.

name: Budget Allocation Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Efficient resource allocation → Systemic: Reduced costs → Strategic: Improved project sustainability.
options: Hierarchical budgeting with clear departmental allocations
Dynamic budgeting based on participant enrollment and research progress
Innovative cost-saving measures through lean operations and supply chain optimization
review: Controls Resource Allocation vs. Project Sustainability. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact of budget cuts on project timelines.

name: Governance Structure Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Clear decision-making authority → Systemic: Improved accountability → Strategic: Enhanced research integrity.
options: Decentralized governance with departmental autonomy
Centralized governance with strong PI oversight
Hybrid governance model with rotating leadership and advisory board input
review: Controls Decision-Making Authority vs. Research Integrity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact of decentralized governance on research coordination.

name: Participant Compensation Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Increased participant recruitment → Systemic: Improved data quality → Strategic: Enhanced research validity.
options: Standardized compensation packages with per-night fees
Variable compensation based on participant engagement and data quality
Innovative compensation models leveraging non-monetary incentives
review: Controls Participant Compensation vs. Research Validity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact of variable compensation on participant bias.

name: Researcher Training Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Improved researcher competency → Systemic: Enhanced data quality → Strategic: Increased research impact.
options: Standardized training protocols with regular refresher courses
Customized training based on researcher role and expertise
Innovative training models leveraging virtual reality and simulation
review: Controls Researcher Competency vs. Data Quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact of customized training on researcher consistency.
